questions.question: Explain the difference between LJMP and SJMP instructions in 8051 with respect to their range and byte size.
keyPoints: LJMP is 3-byte instruction, can access any address in 64KB program memory
SJMP is 2-byte instruction, can only jump to addresses within -128 to +127 bytes from current PC
LJMP provides absolute addressing while SJMP uses relative addressing

questions.question: Describe the working of CJNE instruction and explain how it affects the Carry flag.
keyPoints: CJNE compares two operands without changing them
If first operand < second operand, Carry flag is set (C=1)
If first operand >= second operand, Carry flag is cleared (C=0)
Used for decision making and loop control

questions.question: Write an 8051 assembly program using DJNZ to calculate the sum of numbers from 1 to 10.
keyPoints: Initialize accumulator to 0
Initialize a register (like R0) with count 10
Use DJNZ loop to add numbers sequentially
Result should be 55 (0x37) in accumulator

questions.question: Explain the difference between RET and RETI instructions in 8051.
keyPoints: RET is used to return from subroutines called by LCALL/ACALL
RETI is used to return from interrupt service routines
RETI restores the interrupt enable (IE) flip-flop
Both pop return address from stack into PC

questions.question: What are conditional branch instructions in 8051? List any four conditional branch instructions with their functions.
keyPoints: Conditional branch instructions check specific conditions before jumping
JZ - Jump if Accumulator is Zero
JNZ - Jump if Accumulator is Not Zero
JC - Jump if Carry flag is set
JNC - Jump if Carry flag is not set
DJNZ - Decrement and Jump if Not Zero

questions.question: Explain the purpose of relative addressing in branch instructions of 8051.
keyPoints: Relative addressing uses signed 8-bit offset (-128 to +127)
Offset is added to current Program Counter (PC)
Makes code relocatable - can be placed anywhere in memory
Most conditional jumps and SJMP use relative addressing

questions.question: Explain all unconditional branch instructions in 8051 microcontroller with their byte size and addressing range.
keyPoints: LJMP (Long Jump): 3 bytes, 64KB range, absolute addressing
SJMP (Short Jump): 2 bytes, -128 to +127 bytes, relative addressing
LCALL (Long Call): 3 bytes, 64KB range, calls subroutine anywhere
ACALL (Absolute Call): 2 bytes, 2KB range, calls subroutine nearby
RET: 1 byte, returns from subroutine
RETI: 1 byte, returns from interrupt

questions.question: Write an 8051 assembly language program to find the largest number from an array of 10 numbers stored from internal RAM address 30H onwards. Store the result at RAM location 40H.
keyPoints: Initialize pointer to array start (30H)
Initialize counter and max variable
Load first number as initial max
Loop through array using CJNE/DJNZ
Compare each number with max using CJNE
Update max if larger number found
Store result at 40H

questions.question: Describe the working of bit-addressable conditional branch instructions JB, JNB, JBC in 8051 with examples.
keyPoints: JB (Jump if Bit): Tests specified bit, jumps if bit = 1
JNB (Jump if Not Bit): Tests specified bit, jumps if bit = 0
JBC (Jump if Bit and Clear): Jumps if bit = 1, then clears the bit
Syntax uses bit-addressable locations (20H-2FH) or certain SFRs
Example: JB P1.0, NEXT - jumps if P1.0 = 1

questions.question: Explain the branch instructions used for loop implementation in 8051 with a program example to multiply two 8-bit numbers using repeated addition.
keyPoints: DJNZ used for loop counter
CJNE used for condition checking in loops
Program: Initialize result=0, load multiplicand, load multiplier into counter
Use DJNZ to repeat addition multiplicand times
Result in accumulator after loop completes
Explain each instruction in the program
